schools.summary: The elaborate genealogy signals Saul's coming importance; the Kish/Ner discrepancy is resolved by omitted links, and 'mighty man of power' means a man of property.
Henry: from the smallest tribe, nearly destroyed at Gibeah, Israel's first king 'sprang as a root out of a dry ground' — God gives more honour to the part which lacked.
Clarke takes 'mighty man of power' literally of bodily strength, which accounts for the extraordinary size of the son.
Gill notes that from Benjamin, Jacob's youngest son, came the first king of Israel.

debates.question: What does 'a mighty man of power' say of Kish?

sides.position: a man of property and substance, as in Ruth 2:1
literally a strong man — his physical strength explains Saul's stature
bold in spirit, strong in body, wealthy in estate — all three

schools.summary: The Hebrew means simply 'a young man' (about forty), and in the childhood of nations heroic stature and beauty commended a king to a semi-barbarous people.
Clarke and Wesley: tall stature and fine appearance were highly valued in ancient and eastern kings, and probably contributed to Saul's election.
Henry: nothing is said of Saul's wisdom or piety, only his looks — the king after the people's heart would at least look great, unlike the king after God's own heart.
Gill contrasts the tall Saul of Benjamin with his namesake the apostle, 'a little man' of the same tribe.

debates.question: What is the weight of Saul's imposing appearance?

sides.position: a providentially fitting credential, since ancient peoples prized heroic stature in kings
a silent indictment — the people who wanted grandeur received height and nothing more

schools.summary: Henry: a great man rising from small beginnings and a great event from small occurrences — promotion comes not by chance, but God is the Judge.
Clarke marvels at the train of common occurrences conspiring toward the throne; Wesley notes asses were of great price when simplicity and industry were in fashion among persons of quality.
Ellicott: the horse being forbidden, asses bore judges and nobles; the trusted servant was traditionally identified with Doeg the Edomite.
Gill notes the asses had strayed from the fields where they were kept.

debates.question: How is God's providence in Saul's rise to be characterized?

sides.position: sovereign appointment — God raiseth up the poor out of the dust to set them among princes
a real but judicial providence, advancing a man 'who certainly did not come there by the approbation of God'

schools.summary: The route runs from the hills of Ephraim through Shalisha ('land of the Three') and Shaalim ('hollow of the hand') back into Benjamin — a systematic, fruitless three-day sweep.
Gill: they began with mount Ephraim because it lay contiguous to Benjamin, where the asses might be supposed to have strayed.
Henry: they travelled far and in vain — but the disappointment was itself the appointment, for Saul met with the kingdom he never dreamed of.
Gill: Zuph is the land of Ramathaim-zophim, which the Targum calls simply 'the land in which was the prophet.'
Clarke reports Calmet's reconstruction of the circuit — some twenty-five leagues, a three days' journey on foot.
Saul calculates the moment his father's anxiety will shift from the asses to the searchers; Kitto calls this tender regard a favourable indication of character.
Henry: children should do nothing to grieve or frighten their parents, but be tender of their tenderness.
Henry: the servant had more religion than his master, and most people would rather be told their fortune than their duty.
Ellicott: that a farm-servant thought the seer approachable over lost asses proves how close and friendly Samuel's relation to the people was.
Wesley: acquaintance with God and serviceableness to his kingdom make men truly honourable; the 'peradventure' doubts whether so great a prophet would stoop to such mean matters.

themes: the man of God held in honour

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley: the present reflects the invariable eastern custom of approaching a superior with a gift — respect, gratitude, or support, never a diviner's fee.
Ellicott: gifts were respectful homage to a superior, as Jacob's spicery to Egypt's vizier and Sheba's gifts to Solomon.
Gill: Saul genuinely did not know whether Samuel took money as the fortune-telling sort did — a telling ignorance of Israel's judge.
Henry preserves both readings — some think Saul measured Samuel like the prophets who divined for money, but the usage was rather general custom and natural equity.

debates.question: Does Saul's question about a present imply that prophets took fees?

sides.position: no — it is the universal eastern custom of homage to a superior
Saul may have measured Samuel by mercenary diviners, coming to him as to a fortune-teller

themes: gifts to the man of God

schools.summary: The quarter-shekel was broken bullion, roughly stamped and snapped at need, silver being then rare; the objection once met, the last hindrance is removed.
Clarke: about ninepence — a token of respect rather than an emolument.
Henry: Saul would not own the money in his pocket till the servant offered to bear the charge — most people love a cheap religion.
Gill marks the servant answering Saul 'again': the whole errand is carried forward by the servant's initiative.

themes: the fourth part of a shekel

schools.summary: Clarke: the note betrays a later compiler of Samuel's memoranda; a seer was always a prophet, but the prophet was not always a seer.
Ellicott: a reviser of very high authority, perhaps of Ezra's day, explained the obsolete title roeh, the most ancient of the three Hebrew words for an inspired man.
Gill: to inquire of God is, as the Targum renders, to 'ask doctrine' of him through a man of prophetic spirit.
Henry: those that are prophets must first be seers — who speaks of the things of God must have an insight into them himself.

debates.question: Who inserted the explanatory note about 'seer' and 'prophet'?

sides.position: a later inspired scribe or compiler, perhaps as late as Ezra
possibly Samuel himself, in old age calling his youth 'beforetime'

schools.summary: Gill: 'good is thy word' — Saul approves the plan as generous and feasible, hoping only to learn the way to the asses.
Ellicott argues the unnamed city is Ramah — Samuel's altar, house, and residence all point there — while Keil and Delitzsch hasten the travellers to the decisive meeting.
Henry: Saul never thought of going to the man of God till the servant proposed it, and providence needs no informed consent.
Gill: the city stood on a hill — hence Ramah, 'high and lifted up' — so the strangers climb as the maidens descend to the well.
Henry: Saul's never having seen Samuel forecloses any suspicion of collusion, and the maidens' ready knowledge shows the advantage of living in religious places.
Clarke: the scene disproves the notion that young women were always kept shut up at home.

themes: no collusion between prophet and king
piety reflected in a town's meanest

schools.summary: The breathless, detailed reply marks an eye-witness account; bamah is technically the high place as a place of sacrifice, not any hill.
Wesley: Samuel was newly returned to his house near the altar he had built; Clarke describes the people's sacrifice as a great feast upon offered flesh and notes Samuel's dwelling at Naioth.
Henry: with the tabernacle deprived of the ark, worship at Samuel's altar in Ramah was for the time allowed.
A very early instance of asking a blessing on meals; the Hebrew verb answers to eulogein, the thanksgiving prayer before the sacrificial meal.
Wesley resolves the blessing into thanksgiving and prayer for acceptance; Clarke notes that the man of God alone performs these rites.
Henry: the people would not eat till Samuel came — let the soul first be served, and crave a blessing on your meat before you partake.
Samuel meets them at the very moment of entry, and the historian breaks off to show the meeting was arranged from heaven; Ellicott adopts Ewald's picture of the bashful Saul and the discerning seer.
Henry: the meeting seemed purely accidental, but divine providence ordered it — a sparrow falls not to the ground without our Father.
Gill marks the simple ascent into Ramah on which the whole appointment turns.
'Uncovered the ear' pictures the head-dress pushed aside for a whisper — one of the few direct intimations of how the Spirit communicated with men.
Henry: God spoke in Samuel's ear in token of friendship, for he revealeth his secret to his servants the prophets; revelation is God removing our deafness.
Wesley suggests a still small voice; Clarke honestly owns that how the communication was made we cannot tell.
Gill: God told him in a free, familiar, friendly way, as a secret.

Clarke: Saul is anointed nagid, captain of the Lord's host, in an age when no king was esteemed who was not a warrior; Wesley explains why the dreaded Philistines, not the passing Ammonite flood, are named.
Ellicott reconciles the verse with the victory of Mizpeh: Philistine power was broken but not destroyed, and their raids were the immediate spur of the demand for a king.
Henry: even in wrath God remembers mercy — he calls Israel 'my people' again and again and sends a captain to save them, humouring their cry like a tender mother.
Gill: the man will arrive with no design of his own, merely directed by the providence of God.
God's 'Behold the man' is the silent answer to Samuel's silent question, and the verb atsar — restrain, control — forecasts a stern, severe rule.
Henry: this is the man that shall restrain my people, 'for magistrates are heirs of restraint.'
Clarke: what intimate communion Samuel must have held with his God — a constant familiarity.
Gill: Saul's height and the fixed hour raised the suspicion, but God spoke that Samuel might be left at no uncertainty.
Henry: Samuel made so mean a figure that Saul took him for a porter rather than a prophet — great worth hidden under despicable appearance, and Samuel knew him before he knew Samuel.
Keil and Delitzsch defend the Hebrew 'in the gate' against emendation, while Ellicott thinks the Septuagint's 'in the midst of the city' may be original.
Gill harmonises: the meeting fell either at the door of Samuel's house or within the city gate as the seer passed out.

debates.question: Did the meeting take place 'in the gate' or 'in the midst of the city'?

sides.position: the Hebrew 'in the gate' stands — entry into a town is precisely through its gate
the Septuagint's 'in the midst of the city' may preserve the original reading

schools.summary: Walking before another was a sign of great esteem; the grammar shifts from singular to plural to include the servant in the feast.
Gill: 'I am the seer' was said not in pride but merely for information's sake.
Clarke: the declaration prepared Saul for the communications afterwards made.

debates.question: What is 'all that is in thine heart' which Samuel will tell?

sides.position: the thoughts of his heart generally, disclosed to prove Samuel a prophet
Saul's own secret thoughts and aspirations, to lift him above the asses to higher things

themes: lifting the heart to higher aspirations

schools.summary: Gill: Samuel's unasked knowledge of the asses and their date must at once convince Saul he was a true prophet.
Henry: if a man of God can answer the unasked question, much more doth God himself understand our thoughts afar off.
Wesley: the desire of Israel is the king they long for, entailed on Saul's house unless sin cut off the entail; Clarke says Saul so understood it.
Ellicott, with the Vulgate and Luther: not what Israel desires, but all that Israel possesses of what is desirable, shall be Saul's.

debates.question: What is 'all the desire of Israel'?

sides.position: the kingship Israel is longing for, now settling on Saul and his father's house
all that is precious and desirable in Israel — 'the best in Israel' — promised to him

schools.summary: Wesley: Benjamin, cut to six hundred men, was scarce reckoned an entire tribe; Clarke calls the speech exceedingly modest — 'but who can bear elevation and prosperity?'
Henry: Saul answers like Gideon before him — a humble disposition is a good presage of preferment.
Gill: 'a Benjamite' — a son of the tribe of Benjamin, as the Targum renders it.
The 'parlour' is the lishkah, the chamber at the high place; some thirty of the city's most distinguished men sat down, and Saul and his servant were placed at their head.
Wesley: the chief place was to raise expectation and prepare the people to give Saul the honour his approaching dignity required.
Henry: though Saul's advancement was Samuel's deposing, the prophet was the first and forwardest man to do him honour.
Gill identifies the room as the dining chamber belonging to the high place.
Everything, down to the reserved dish, was foreseen and provided for — all symbolical of the coming constitutional change; what lay on the leg was the attached fat, not broth or kidney.
Clarke: the Hebrew tabbach is properly the butcher — the slaughterer of the sacrifice kept charge of its portions.
Gill: the cook is simply the one who dressed and prepared the food for the guests.
Following Payne Smith, the Hebrew makes the cook, not Samuel, the speaker — his chat shows Saul how completely his coming had been foreseen.

Clarke: the shoulder was an emblem of the government now laid on Saul — 'the government shall be upon his shoulder'; Wesley keeps Samuel as speaker, ordering the portion when he first invited the people.
Henry: the left shoulder was Samuel's own accustomed mess, so giving it to Saul was an implicit resignation of his place — the shoulder for strength, the breast for affection.
Gill: in those unsettled times Samuel may have officiated as priest, and Saul, fed from the priest's portion, learns to live in friendship with the priests of the Lord.

debates.question: Who speaks the words over the reserved shoulder?

sides.position: the cook — Samuel's name is absent from the Hebrew, and the verb is a participle
Samuel himself, as the Authorised Version, Septuagint and Vulgate read
an emblem of government and strength — the burden of rule laid on the king
simply the choicest portion, the leg with its sacrificial fat, honourably reserved

themes: a destiny kept unto the appointed time

schools.summary: Clarke describes the flat eastern roof as the place of evening converse; Wesley says plainly they communed concerning the kingdom designed for Saul by God.
Henry imagines Samuel telling the whole story of the people's demand and assuring Saul of his willingness to resign.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott hold the evening talk was preparation only — on Israel's decline and the need of an obedient leader — the royal word being reserved for the morning.
Gill traces the descent from the high place and the ascent to the hill-built city.

debates.question: What did Samuel and Saul discuss on the housetop?

sides.position: the kingdom itself, designed for Saul by God
only preparation — Israel's religious and political state — since the word of Jehovah waited for the morrow

themes: the outgoing judge and the incoming king

schools.summary: The two clauses narrate one event in Hebrew fashion — they rose at dawn, Samuel calling from below to the roof where Saul slept on the balcony.
Clarke: Saul had slept on the housetop all night, and at break of day Samuel called him up.
Gill passes on Abarbinel's conjecture that neither man could sleep — Samuel for what he must do, Saul for the hints and honours of the day.

sides.position: soundly and heavily, weary after the day's excitements
neither Samuel nor Saul could sleep at all, so full were their minds

themes: last morning of a private life

schools.summary: Wesley: the privacy guarded Samuel from the charge of imposing a king, that the lot might show the kingdom given by God's destination, not the prophet's contrivance; Clarke defines the word as the counsel or design of the Lord.
Henry: Samuel would not jest with the sacred rite of anointing — and Saul, seeking asses, literally found a kingdom, as many seeking the world have found salvation.
Ellicott: the meaning of all that had happened was now to be revealed, and when God imposes new duties he never forgets to bestow new powers.
Gill notes the descent to the city's end, where the private word could be spoken.

themes: the word of God shown privately
